title: Experimenting with music — creating
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to the IB Music HL 'Experimenting with music — creating' component. It deconstructs the assessment criteria, offers practical strategies for systematic experimentation, and provides models for documenting the creative process effectively to meet the demands of the top markbands.
learningObjectives: Understand and apply the assessment criteria for the Experimenting portfolio (Criteria A, B, C, D).
Develop a clear and compelling musical intention to guide the creative process.
Implement systematic and wide-ranging experimental strategies in composition or performance creation.
Master the skill of documenting the creative journey through a clear, coherent, and compelling experimentation report.
Refine musical ideas and synthesise them into a coherent final piece that demonstrates technical and expressive skill.

simpleExplanation.title: The Composer's Laboratory
simpleExplanation.summary: The 'Experimenting' portfolio is not just about writing a piece of music; it's about proving you've been on a creative journey. Examiners want to see your thought process: the ideas you tried, the problems you solved, and the reasons for your final musical decisions.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of yourself as a sonic chef developing a new signature dish. You don't just throw ingredients into a pot. You start with an idea (e.g., 'a spicy, yet refreshing, soup'). You then experiment: trying different chillies for heat, different herbs for freshness, adjusting the cooking time. You taste, you take notes on what works and what doesn't, and you refine the recipe until it perfectly matches your original vision. Your experimentation report is your recipe book, explaining the journey from initial concept to final, delicious dish.
steps: Define a specific musical intention: What story, mood, or concept do you want to express? Write this down as a clear, focused statement.
Explore possibilities systematically: Isolate musical parameters (melody, harmony, rhythm, timbre) and experiment with each. Try contrasting ideas and techniques, even ones outside your comfort zone.

Document your process critically: For every experiment, record what you did, what the result was, and—most importantly—how it relates to your musical intention. Use score excerpts, audio clips, and analytical language.

Synthesise and refine: Select the most successful experimental outcomes and weave them into a coherent musical work. Polish the details to ensure the final piece is a convincing realisation of your initial intention.

flashcards.back: Assesses the extent to which you have explored a range of musical material and ideas. Top marks require 'systematic', 'imaginative', and 'wide-ranging' exploration that is clearly documented.

Assesses the clarity of your musical intention and how effectively you develop your ideas towards it. Top marks are for a 'clear and compelling' intention with 'logical, coherent, and imaginative' development.

flashcards.front: Criterion C: Technical and expressive skills
flashcards.back: Assesses the quality of the final music. For creating, this means your 'highly effective' and 'assured' use of musical elements and 'idiomatic' writing for the chosen medium.

Assesses the clarity and coherence of your experimentation report. Top marks require a 'clear, coherent, and compelling' report that effectively communicates the creative journey with appropriate evidence.
The specific artistic goal or concept behind your musical work. It should be more than just a title; it's a statement of purpose that guides all your creative decisions.

Creating music that is well-suited to the technical and expressive capabilities of the chosen instrument(s) or voice(s). It 'fits' the instrument naturally and allows the performer to play it effectively.
The process of combining different musical ideas, techniques, and experimental outcomes into a unified and coherent whole. It's the opposite of a collection of disconnected fragments.
The late-stage process of polishing and improving musical material. This involves small adjustments to harmony, orchestration, articulation, and structure to enhance clarity and impact.

A methodical and organised approach to experimentation, rather than random trial and error. For example, exploring all possible inversions of a chord, or applying a rhythmic motive to different melodic contours.
Analysing your own creative process and outcomes, evaluating their effectiveness against your musical intention, and justifying your decisions. It answers the 'why' question, not just the 'what'.
'Competent' (mid-markband) implies the music works on a basic level. 'Coherent' (top-markband) implies that all the musical parts work together to create a convincing and unified artistic statement.

Refers to the materials you include to support your written commentary. This includes musical score excerpts (handwritten or engraved), annotated screenshots from a DAW, short audio/video clips, and diagrams.

faq.q: How 'finished' or long does my created piece need to be?
faq.a: The guide specifies 3-6 minutes of music. However, quality is far more important than quantity. A highly developed and coherent 3-minute piece that perfectly realises its intention is much better than a rambling, unfocused 6-minute piece. Focus on creating a complete and convincing musical statement.

faq.q: Can I use a DAW and plugins, or must I use traditional notation?
faq.a: You can use any medium for creation: notation software, a DAW, live electronics, or even graphic notation. The key is that you must be able to document your process and submit your final work effectively. For DAW-based work, this means submitting an audio file and using screenshots and audio excerpts as evidence in your report.

faq.q: What if my experiments 'fail' or lead to a dead end?
faq.a: Documenting so-called 'failures' is excellent evidence for Criterion A (Exploration) and B (Development). It demonstrates critical reflection. Explain what you tried, why it did not work in the context of your musical intention, and how this 'failed' experiment led you to a more successful solution. This shows a mature and rigorous creative process.

faq.q: Can my musical intention change during the process?
faq.a: Yes, a musical intention can evolve. This is a natural part of the creative process. However, you must document this evolution in your report. Explain the initial intention, what discovery or experiment prompted a change in direction, and what the new, refined intention became. This demonstrates adaptability and reflection.

sections.content: Welcome to the Experimenting component for Music HL. This is one of the most creative and personal parts of the course, where you get to be the composer or creator. It is assessed through a portfolio consisting of your finished musical work(s) and a detailed experimentation report. Success in this component hinges on your ability to not only create compelling music but also to articulate the creative journey that led to it. Examiners are assessing your process as much as your product, rewarding systematic exploration, critical reflection, and the clear realisation of a musical intention.
Deconstructing the Assessment Criteria for Creating
To achieve the highest marks (Band 7), your work must demonstrate excellence across four distinct criteria. Understanding these is the first step to success.

items: **Criterion A: Exploration and discovery (8 marks)**: This is about the 'what if?' stage. You must show evidence of 'imaginative and wide-ranging' exploration. This means trying out significantly different ideas, not just minor variations. Your exploration must also be 'systematic'—that is, logical and purposeful, not random. Your report must clearly document this process.

**Criterion B: Intention and development (8 marks)**: Your project must be guided by a 'clear and compelling' musical intention. This is your artistic vision. The development of your music from initial sketches to the final piece must be 'logical and coherent', showing a clear path of decision-making that consistently serves your intention.

**Criterion C: Technical and expressive skills (8 marks)**: This criterion judges the final music itself. Examiners look for 'highly effective' and 'assured' handling of musical parameters (harmony, melody, rhythm, structure, timbre). Your writing should be 'idiomatic' for the chosen instruments/voices, and the overall result should be a 'coherent and expressive' piece of music.

**Criterion D: Presentation and communication (6 marks)**: This assesses your experimentation report. It must be a 'clear, coherent, and compelling' narrative of your creative process. It should integrate written commentary with well-chosen evidence (score excerpts, audio clips) to effectively communicate your journey of exploration, discovery, and refinement.

sections.question: In your experimentation report, document a key moment where you experimented with rhythmic devices to create a sense of instability, in line with your musical intention to portray 'a fractured dreamscape'.
sections.solution:
My initial rhythmic approach for the main theme (see Excerpt A) was a simple 4/4 ostinato in the cello. While functional, it felt too stable and predictable, failing to capture the disorienting quality of a 'fractured dreamscape'. To address this, I began a systematic exploration of rhythmic disruption. My first experiment involved polymetre, layering a 3/4 pattern in the viola against the cello's 4/4. This created some tension, but the regular cycles became predictable after a few bars. 

Seeking a more unpredictable solution, I experimented with metric modulation and irrational tuplets, inspired by the music of Thomas Adès. I took the original four-note cello motive and applied a quintuplet grouping across the beat (Excerpt B), creating a sense of acceleration and forward tumbling. I then fragmented this idea, inserting rests and displacing accents to avoid establishing a clear pulse. The final version (Excerpt C) combines this fragmented quintuplet idea with sudden shifts to a slower, augmented version of the motive in 5/8 time. This juxtaposition of frantic energy and sudden stasis proved highly effective. It avoids a stable pulse, creating the desired rhythmic instability and successfully realising my intention to build a disorienting and 'fractured' sonic environment.

sections.content: Your musical intention is the conceptual anchor for your entire project. It is the 'why' that drives your creative choices. A weak intention like 'a sad piano piece' offers little guidance. A strong intention provides a framework for experimentation and a benchmark against which to measure success.

items: **Weak Intention**: 'A fast piece for violin and piano.'

**Strong Intention**: 'To create a virtuosic moto perpetuo for violin and piano that explores the tension between mechanical precision and human fallibility, using minimalist-style repeating cells that gradually deconstruct and fragment.'

**Strong Intention**: 'To compose a song for voice and guitar that captures the five stages of grief following a breakup, with each musical section corresponding to a stage: denial (static harmony), anger (dissonant, aggressive strumming), bargaining (shifting modalities), depression (sparse texture, low tessitura), and acceptance (simple, diatonic melody).'

sections.content: Write your musical intention at the very beginning of your report. Refer back to it explicitly throughout your commentary. For example, 'This harmonic choice was more effective in achieving the sense of 'vast emptiness' stated in my intention because...'

To demonstrate 'wide-ranging' and 'systematic' exploration, you must move beyond simply tweaking a few notes. Employ concrete strategies to generate and transform your material. This is your research and development phase.

items: **Parameter Isolation**: Focus on one musical element at a time. Take a simple melody and experiment *only* with its rhythm. Then, take the original melody and experiment *only* with its harmonisation (e.g., try diatonic, chromatic, modal, and quartal harmony).

**Algorithmic & Chance Processes**: Use a set of rules or chance to generate material. For example, assign pitches to numbers and use a random number generator, or create a 'tone row' and apply serialist transformations (inversion, retrograde). This forces you out of your habitual patterns.

**Technological Manipulation**: Use your Digital Audio Workstation (DAW) as an experimental tool. Record a simple sound and radically transform it using time-stretching, granular synthesis, spectral processing, or complex effects chains. Document the process with screenshots and parameter settings.

**Constraint-Based Creating**: Set strict limitations for yourself. For example, compose a section using only three pitches, or write a passage for piano without using the sustain pedal. Constraints often foster immense creativity by forcing you to find novel solutions.

sections.question: Explain how you used a DAW to experiment with timbre to create an 'ethereal and otherworldly' texture for your electronic piece.
sections.solution:
My intention was to create an 'ethereal and otherworldly' soundscape. I began by recording a single, simple sine wave. In Ableton Live, I loaded this into the 'Granulator II' synthesiser. My experimentation focused on manipulating three key parameters to transform the simple tone into a complex texture. 

First, I increased the 'Spray' parameter, which introduces random pitch variations to the grains. This immediately diffused the solid pitch into a shimmering, cloud-like sonority. Second, I used a very long 'Grain Size' with a high degree of randomisation. This created overlapping sonic events that blurred together, removing any sense of clear attack and contributing to the ethereal quality. 

Finally, and most effectively, I automated the 'File Position' parameter to slowly scan back and forth across the audio file (even though it was a static sine wave, this created subtle phasing artefacts). An annotated screenshot of my Granulator II settings is provided (see Figure 1). The resulting sound was a constantly shifting, shimmering texture that perfectly matched my intention. I then layered three instances of this patch at different octaves, panning them widely to create an immersive, otherworldly atmosphere that a simple synth pad could not achieve.

sections.content: Your report (max 1,500 words) is not a diary of every single thing you did. It is a curated, analytical narrative that persuades the examiner of the quality of your process. It must be selective, reflective, and well-evidenced.

items: **Structure is Key**: Organise your report thematically (e.g., 'Experimenting with Harmony', 'Developing the Structure') rather than purely chronologically. This allows for a more focused and analytical discussion.

**Integrate Evidence Seamlessly**: Don't just place score excerpts randomly. Refer to them directly in your text (e.g., 'As shown in Excerpt 2.1...'). Annotate your evidence to highlight the specific musical feature you are discussing.
**Use Precise Musical Language**: Demonstrate your understanding by using correct terminology (e.g., 'modal interchange', 'hemiola', 'subtractive EQ', 'hocketing').

**Focus on the 'Why'**: For every decision you describe, justify it in relation to your musical intention. The best reports read like a persuasive argument, proving that the final music is the logical and creative outcome of a rigorous experimental process.

sections.label: Test Your Knowledge of Experimenting & Creating
